1: Qĕṭûrāh — "Keturah," Abraham's second wife taken after Sarah's death. The name likely derives from a root related to incense or smoke (qāṭar, to burn incense). The narrative introduces a tension about her status: here she is called ʾiššāh (wife), but v6 and 1 Chr 1:32 call her a pîlegeš (concubine). The distinction matters legally: a concubine's sons receive gifts (mattānōt, v6) rather than full inheritance. The narrator may preserve two traditions or signal that Keturah held an intermediate status — a secondary wife rather than a slave-concubine like Hagar.
2: The six sons of Keturah — Zimran, Jokshan, Medan, Midyān (Midian), Ishbak, Shuah — are the genealogical founders of Arabian tribal peoples. Midian becomes particularly significant: the Midianites are enemies of Israel (Num 31; Judg 6-8) but also the family of Moses's father-in-law Jethro (Exod 2:15-22; 3:1). The genealogy places these peoples within the Abrahamic family while explaining why they stand outside the covenant line. The list is formulaic — paratactic names linked by waw — characteristic of genealogical registers throughout Genesis.
3: Yoqšān wālad ʾet-Šeḇāʾ wĕʾet-Dĕḏān — "Jokshan fathered Sheba and Dedan." Sheba (H7614, from šāḇaʿ, seven/oath) is the source of the Sabaean people of Arabia, famous for the Queen of Sheba episode (1 Kgs 10:1-13). Dedan appears in Isaiah (21:13; Isa 60:6) and Ezekiel (27:20; 38:13) as a trading people. The second-generation names Ashurites, Letushites, and Leummites are otherwise unattested — likely small Arabian tribal groups. The genealogy shows that Abraham's blessing extended beyond the covenant line to produce nations throughout the ancient world.
4: Bĕnê Miḏyān — "sons of Midian." The five Midianite sub-groups listed here (Ephah, Epher, Hanoch, Abida, Eldaah) are names that appear in later commercial and political contexts. Ephah is named in Isa 60:6 among the nations bringing gifts of gold and frankincense to Zion. The genealogical formula kol-ʾēlleh bĕnê qĕṭûrāh ("all these were sons of Keturah") closes the section — the same closing formula used for Ishmael's sons in v16 (kol-ʾēlleh bĕnê yišmāʿēl). Both sections frame the non-covenant lines that nonetheless descend from Abraham.
5: wayyittēn ʾaḇrāhām ʾet-kol-ʾăšer-lô lĕyiṣḥāq — "Abraham gave everything he had to Isaac." The verb nātan (gave) combined with kol-ʾăšer-lô (all that he had) is the formulaic language of inheritance transfer (cf. 24:36). This legal act is completed while Abraham is still alive — a form of inter vivos transfer rather than testamentary bequest. The exclusivity of Isaac's inheritance (ʾet-kol, everything) establishes the unambiguous covenant line before Abraham dies, eliminating any dispute after his death about which son is the primary heir.
6: mattānōt — "gifts." The noun mattānāh (H4976) = a gift, something presented rather than owed as a legal right, distinguishing the concubines' sons' portion from Isaac's yeruššāh (inheritance). Abraham sends them qēḏmāh (eastward) — the direction of non-covenant peoples in Genesis: Cain goes east (4:16), the tower builders are in the east (11:2), Lot chooses the east (13:11), and the sons of Keturah settle east. The east is the direction away from the covenant land. Bĕʿôḏennû ḥāy — "while he was still living" — the disposition during lifetime contrasts with inheritance received at death; Abraham settles matters before he dies.
7: wĕʾēlleh yĕmê šnê-ḥayyê ʾaḇrāhām — "These are the days of the years of Abraham's life." The life-summary formula for Abraham (175 years) parallels the genealogical summaries of the pre-flood patriarchs (5:3-32) and Ishmael's summary (v17). The explicit counting of years connects Abraham to the covenant-history framework of Genesis time. 175 = 5 × 5 × 7, which later Jewish tradition noted as a "perfect" number, though the text does not make this observation — the narrator simply records the completeness of the life without embellishment.
8: wayyiggwaʿ wayyāmat ʾaḇrāhām bĕśêḇāh ṭôḇāh zāqēn wĕśāḇēʿa wayyēʾāsep ʾel-ʿammāyw — "Abraham breathed his last and died in good old age, an old man and full of years, and was gathered to his people." The verb gāwaʿ (H1478, to expire, breathe one's last) — a rare verb used predominantly for death in Genesis (25:8,17; 35:29; 49:33) — differs from the common mût (to die); it emphasizes the physical cessation of breath. Śāḇēʿa (satisfied, full) — from śāḇaʿ, to be satisfied/sated — describes a life brought to its natural completion without deficit. Wayyēʾāsep ʾel-ʿammāyw — "gathered to his people" — a distinctive formula for honorable death within the covenant community (also used for Ishmael, 25:17; Isaac, 35:29; Jacob, 49:33; Aaron, Num 20:24; Moses, Num 27:13). The idiom implies existence beyond death — one is gathered to those who preceded him — though the text does not elaborate the mode of that existence.
9: wayyiqbĕrû ʾōtô yiṣḥāq wĕyišmāʿēl bānāyw — "Isaac and Ishmael his sons buried him." The two sons who have been separated since Ishmael's expulsion (ch 21) come together for their father's burial — a reunion the text records without editorial comment. The cave of Machpelah (mĕʿārat hammakpēlāh), purchased by Abraham in Gen 23 from Ephron the Hittite, now fulfills its purpose as the patriarchal burial place. The land purchase was specifically for this — ownership of a burial plot was the tangible beginning of the covenant inheritance in Canaan. Jacob and Leah will also be buried here (49:29-32; 50:13).
10: šĕḏēh qānāh ʾaḇrāhām mēʾēt bĕnê-ḥēt šāmmāh qubbar ʾaḇrāhām wĕśārāh ʾišĕtô — "the field Abraham had purchased from the Hittites — there Abraham was buried, and Sarah his wife." The burial of Abraham with Sarah is the completion of the Machpelah transaction (ch 23). The field (śāḏeh) is specifically the field; the narrator uses the legal term qānāh (purchased, acquired) that was used in the formal transaction of 23:16-18. Abraham's burial "with Sarah his wife" ties the covenant pair together in death as in life — the couple who received the promise together rest together in the covenant land.
11: wayĕḇāreḵ ʾĕlōhîm ʾet-yiṣḥāq bĕnô wayyēšeḇ yiṣḥāq ʿim-bĕʾēr laḥay rōʾî — "God blessed Isaac his son, and Isaac settled near Beer-lahai-roi." The first act after Abraham's death is God's blessing of Isaac — the covenant transfers immediately without interruption. Bĕʾēr laḥay rōʾî ("well of the Living One who sees me") is the well named by Hagar in 16:14 (ʾattāh ʾēl rōʾî, "you are the God who sees"). Isaac settles precisely where Hagar encountered God — the geography is theologically charged: the covenant heir dwells at the place named for the God who sees and provides.
12: wĕʾēlleh tôlĕḏōt yišmāʿēl ben-ʾaḇrāhām — "These are the generations of Ishmael son of Abraham." The tôlĕḏōt formula (H8435, generations/account) marks a major structural division in Genesis — the tenth and final use of the formula before the Jacob cycle begins. The Ishmael genealogy is placed here, between Abraham's death and the Isaac-Jacob narrative, to close out the non-covenant line completely before the covenant story resumes. The specification "son of Abraham, whom Hagar the Egyptian, Sarah's slave, bore to Abraham" rehearses the full genealogical and social context — emphasizing both the Abrahamic connection and the slave-mother status that excluded him from the primary inheritance.
13: šĕmôt bĕnê yišmāʿēl bišmōtām lĕtôlĕḏōtām — "the names of the sons of Ishmael by their names, by their generations." The twelve sons of Ishmael listed in vv13-15 fulfill the promise of 17:20 ("twelve princes shall he father"). The firstborn Nĕḇāyôt (Nebaioth) is linked with Kedar in Isa 60:7 as bringing offerings to YHWH in the eschatological gathering of nations. The number twelve is structurally significant: Ishmael's twelve princes parallel the twelve tribes of Israel (35:22-26) — God's promise to Ishmael is given the same numerical form as his promise to Jacob.
14: The names Mishma, Dumah, Massa appear in the list with no commentary in the text. Dumah (H1746, silence) becomes the name of an Edomite region and appears in Isa 21:11 as a place of prophetic oracle. Massa appears in Prov 30:1 and 31:1 as the oracle-source of Agur and Lemuel — if these are Ishmaelite tribal names, two of Proverbs' wisdom contributors may be descendants of Ishmael, broadening the wisdom tradition beyond Israel. The genealogical names are terse but their connections to later OT material give them weight beyond the list itself.
15: The final four sons of Ishmael — Hadad, Tema, Jetur, Naphish, Kedemah — represent Arabian tribes documented in Assyrian inscriptions. Tema appears in Job 6:19 and Isa 21:14 as an oasis city and caravan stop. Jetur and Naphish reappear in 1 Chr 5:19-20 as peoples against whom the Transjordanian tribes warred. The specificity of the genealogical names connects the biblical text to the identifiable political geography of the ancient Near East — these are not literary inventions but remembered peoples.
16: šĕnêm ʿāśār nĕśîʾim lĕʾummōtām — "twelve princes according to their clans." Nāśîʾ (H5387, prince, chief, one who lifts/carries) — from the root nāśāʾ (to lift up), signifying a leader who carries responsibility for the group. The word is used for tribal leaders throughout the Pentateuch (Num 1:16; 7:2,10; 34:18). Lĕʾummōtām (according to their clans/peoples) — leʾōm (H3816, a people, clan) appears in the oracle to Rebekah in v23 (ûšĕnê leʾummîm, "two peoples"); the same word is used for Ishmael's descendants and for Jacob/Esau in the womb — suggesting the structural parallelism between the twelve Ishmaelite princes and the twelve Israelite tribes.
17: wayyiggwaʿ wayyāmat wayyēʾāsep ʾel-ʿammāyw — "he breathed his last and died and was gathered to his people." Ishmael's death formula repeats Abraham's almost verbatim (v8): gāwaʿ (expire), mût (die), neeaph ʾel-ʿammāyw (gathered to his people). The identical formula for father and son suggests that Ishmael's end, though outside the covenant line, is an honorable death — the same language of completion is applied. Ishmael's 137 years (vs. Abraham's 175) marks him as fully flourishing without the covenant's full weight. The gathering-to-his-people language implies the same post-mortem continuity as Abraham's death.
18: wayyiškon mēḥăwîlāh ʿad-šûr ʾăšer ʿal-pĕnê miṣrayim bōʾăḵāh ʾaššûrāh — "He settled from Havilah to Shur, which is opposite Egypt, going toward Assyria." The territory described (from Havilah to Shur) matches the wilderness east of Egypt — cf. 1 Sam 15:7 for the same description of Saul's campaign against the Amalekites. ʿAl-pĕnê kol-ʾeḥāyw nāpāl — "He settled in hostility to all his brothers" — the verb nāpal (to fall, settle, live) in the phrase ʿal-pĕnê (over against, in the face of) echoes the earlier oracle about Ishmael: yādô bakkōl wĕyad kōl bô ("his hand against everyone, everyone's hand against him," 16:12). The fulfillment of the wilderness-dweller oracle closes the Ishmael genealogy.
19: wĕʾēlleh tôlĕḏōt yiṣḥāq ben-ʾaḇrāhām ʾaḇrāhām hôlîḏ ʾet-yiṣḥāq — "These are the generations of Isaac son of Abraham — Abraham fathered Isaac." The tôlĕḏōt formula marks the beginning of the Isaac-Jacob narrative as the dominant structural unit. The doubling "Isaac son of Abraham — Abraham fathered Isaac" is often noted as tautological, but it is a deliberate rhetorical emphasis: the covenant line runs specifically through the father-son chain of Abraham → Isaac, not through the Keturah or Hagar lines. The repetition drives home the genealogical and covenantal specificity.
20: wĕyiṣḥāq ben-ʾarbāʿîm šānāh bĕqaḥtô ʾet-riqāh bat-bĕtûʾēl hāʾărammî mippaddān ʾărām ʾăḥôt lāḇān hāʾărammî lô lĕʾiššāh — "Isaac was forty years old when he took Rebekah daughter of Bethuel the Aramean from Paddan Aram, sister of Laban the Aramean, as his wife." The age-marker (40 years) is the narrator's anchor for chronological reckoning. The triple repetition of "Aramean" (ʾărammî) in one verse identifies Rebekah's ethnic origin emphatically — she is from outside Canaan, from the ancestral homeland of the patriarchs in Aram-Naharaim. The specification "sister of Laban the Aramean" introduces Laban, who will be Jacob's antagonist in the Jacob cycle (chs 29-31). Laban's identity is established before Jacob is born.
21: wayyeʿtar yiṣḥāq laYHWH lĕnokaḥ ʾišĕtô kî ʿăqārāh hîʾ — "Isaac prayed to YHWH on behalf of his wife, because she was barren." The verb ʿātar (H6279, to pray, entreat, intercede) is not the common prayer verb pālal or the root of tĕpillāh; ʿātar has a specifically intercessory, entreating nuance — it appears for Moses's plague intercession (Exod 8:8-13,25-30; 9:28-33; 10:17-18) and prophetic intercession (1 Sam 8:6; 12:19). The verb places Isaac in the role of intercessor for his barren wife, as Abraham was for Abimelech's barren household (20:17). The barrenness motif (ʿăqārāh, H6135, barren, one who is rootless/childless) connects Sarah, Rebekah, Rachel, Hannah, Elizabeth — the line of covenant continuity runs through women whose barrenness is overcome by divine intervention, not natural fertility.
22: wayyitroṣṣĕṣû habbānîm bĕqirbāh — "The children struggled within her." The Hithpolel of rāṣaṣ (H7533, to crush, bruise, oppress) — an unusual verbal form suggesting violent struggle, not mere movement; the Hithpolel adds reciprocity: they were crushing each other. The word is used in Judg 9:53 for crushing a skull and in Eccl 12:6 for a wheel broken. Rebekah's distress is linguistically grounded: this is not normal fetal movement. Wattēleḵ liḏrōš ʾet-YHWH — "and she went to inquire of YHWH." The verb dāraš (H1875, to seek, inquire, consult) is the standard term for consulting a divine oracle or prophet (1 Sam 9:9; 1 Kgs 22:8; 2 Kgs 8:8; Isa 55:6). Rebekah's inquiry is a prophetic consultation; the oracle she receives (v23) is the interpretive key for the entire Jacob-Esau narrative.
23: šĕnê gôyim bĕḇiṭnēḵ ûšĕnê leʾummîm mimmēʿayiḵ yippārēḏû ûleʾōm miḵleʾōm yeʾĕmāṣ wĕraḇ yaʿăḇōḏ ṣāʿîr — "Two nations are in your womb, two peoples shall be separated from within you; one people shall be stronger than the other, and the older shall serve the younger." The oracle uses both gôyim (nations, H1471 — foreign peoples, Gentiles) and leʾummîm (peoples, H3816 — a rarer, more poetic term); the pair creates a formal parallelism. Yippārēḏû (Niphal imperfect of pāraḏ, to be separated) — from within the womb, separation is already decreed. The climactic line wĕraḇ yaʿăḇōḏ ṣāʿîr — "the elder/great shall serve the younger/small" — is the oracle's punch: raḇ (great, elder, older) will serve ṣāʿîr (small, younger, lesser). Paul cites this oracle in Rom 9:10-12 as the clearest OT statement of unconditional election: the oracle is given before the twins are born, "not because of works but because of him who calls."
24: wĕhinnēh tôʾmîm bĕḇiṭnāh — "And behold, there were twins in her womb." The exclamatory hinnēh ("behold!") marks this as the fulfillment-surprise: Rebekah received a word about two nations, and now the physical reality is revealed — twins. The word tĕʾômîm (H8380, twins) — from the root tāʾam (to be double, to be paired) — is used here and in 38:27 (Tamar's twins Perez and Zerah). The twin births in Genesis (Jacob/Esau, Perez/Zerah) both involve reversals of the firstborn's rights — Esau's birthright passes to Jacob, and Perez pushes past Zerah in the birth canal. The pattern of twin-births-with-reversals is structurally significant in the covenant genealogy.
25: admōnî kullô kĕʾadderet śēʿār wayyiqrĕʾû šĕmô ʿēśāw — "The first came out red/ruddy, all of him like a hairy mantle, and they called his name Esau." Two wordplays operate simultaneously: (1) admōnî (ruddy, reddish, H132) plays on ʾĕḏôm (Edom, H123), the name of Esau's descendants and the land they inhabit; (2) śēʿār (hairy, H8181) plays on Śēʿîr (Seir, H8165), the mountainous region that becomes Edom's homeland. Both wordplays are made explicit in 25:30 (Edom = red) and 32:3/36:8 (Seir = Esau's territory). The name ʿēśāw itself is of uncertain etymology — "made/done" from ʿāśāh (to make/do) has been proposed, but this is not certain.
26: wĕyādô ʾōḥezet bĕʿăqēḇ ʿēśāw wayyiqrāʾ šĕmô yaʿăqōḇ — "and his hand had grasped Esau's heel, and his name was called Jacob." ʾĒqeḇ (H6119, heel) — the name Jacob (yaʿăqōḇ) is a paronomasia (wordplay) on this word: yaʿăqōḇ = "one who seizes the heel" or, from the related verb ʿāqaḇ (H6117), "one who supplants/trips up/circumvents by the heel." The two semantic valences (heel-holder vs. supplanter) are both operative: the physical act of holding the heel in the womb becomes the characteristic description of Jacob's method of operating — grasping what belongs to another by cunning rather than by right. Esau makes this explicit in 27:36: wayyaʿqĕḇēnî zeh paʿămayim — "has he not supplanted me these two times?" — using the verb ʿāqaḇ. Hosea 12:3 picks up the wordplay: "In the womb he took his brother by the heel, and in his manhood he strove with God."
27: wayyiggĕḏal hanaʿarîm wayhî ʿēśāw ʾîš yōḏēaʿ ṣayid ʾîš śāḏeh wĕyaʿăqōḇ ʾîš tām yōšēḇ ʾohālîm — "the boys grew up and Esau became a skillful hunter, a man of the field, while Jacob was a quiet man, dwelling in tents." The characterizations are carefully crafted: yōḏēaʿ ṣayid ("knower of hunt") uses yāḏaʿ (H3045, to know) in a skilled-mastery sense. Esau is man of the open country (śāḏeh, field/open land) — the same word for the uncultivated land contrasted with settled cultivation. Jacob is tām (H8535, complete, blameless, whole) — the same word for Noah (6:9) and Abraham (17:1: tāmîm); not moral perfection but integrity of character. Yōšēḇ ʾohālîm ("tent-dweller") marks Jacob as the pastoral, settled type, contrasting with Esau's roving hunter character.
28: wayyeʾĕhab yiṣḥāq ʾet-ʿēśāw kî-ṣayid bĕpîw wĕriqāh ʾōheḇet ʾet-yaʿăqōḇ — "Isaac loved Esau, for game was in his mouth, but Rebekah loved Jacob." The verb ʾāhaḇ (H157, to love) is used for the parental love that generates the family conflict of the next chapters. The motivation for Isaac's love is startling: kî-ṣayid bĕpîw — "because game was in his mouth" — the love is grounded in sensory pleasure (wild game). This parallels the materiality of the birthright scene in vv29-34 where Esau's physical hunger drives a covenant decision. Rebekah's love for Jacob lacks this kind of appetitive explanation — the narrator leaves her motive unspecified, which may suggest it was grounded in the oracle she received (v23).
29: wayyāzeḏ yaʿăqōḇ nāzîḏ wayyāḇōʾ ʿēśāw min-haśśāḏeh wĕhûʾ ʿāyēp — "Jacob was cooking stew when Esau came in from the field, and he was exhausted." Nāzîḏ (H5138, stew/broth/pottage) — from the root zîḏ (H2102, to boil, to be presumptuous/arrogant); the same root is used in Exod 18:11 and Neh 9:10,16 for "presumptuously" acting. The double meaning (cooked presumptuous broth) may be a deliberate wordplay — Jacob's cooking is itself an act of presumptuous calculation. ʿĀyēp (H5889, exhausted, faint, weary) — physically drained from the hunt; Esau's physical state will govern his decision. The contrast between Jacob's indoor cooking and Esau's outdoor hunting sets up the exchange.
30: haʾăṭîṭēnî nāʾ min-hāʾāḏōm hāʾāḏōm hazzeh kî ʿāyēp ʾānōḵî — "Let me eat some of that red stuff — that red stuff — for I am exhausted!" The demand is grammatically crude: haʾăṭîṭēnî = "let me swallow" (H3938, a rare verb for gulping/swallowing quickly) — not "give me" but "pour it into me." The reduplication hāʾāḏōm hāʾāḏōm (the red, the red) shows impatience and tunnel-vision; Esau does not even name what he wants — just "that red stuff." ʿal-kēn qārāʾ-šĕmô ʾĕḏôm — "therefore his name was called Edom" — the etiological note makes the link explicit: Esau = Edom; the red demand = the red people. The wordplay is phonological: ʾāḏōm (red) → ʾĕḏôm (Edom).
31: miḵrāh kāyôm ʾet-bĕḵōrātĕḵā lî — "Sell me today your birthright." Bĕḵōrāh (H1062, birthright, primogeniture right) — the legal bundle of rights belonging to the firstborn, including double inheritance (Deut 21:17 specifies the double portion) and family leadership authority. The imperative miḵrāh (sell!) is from māḵar (H4376, to sell, transfer ownership by transaction). Jacob frames the exchange as a commercial transaction. The word kāyôm ("today") specifies immediacy — the sale is to be consummated now, not deferred. Jacob's timing is calculated: Esau is vulnerable in his exhaustion and hunger.
32: hinnēh ʾānōḵî hôlēḵ lāmût wĕlāmmāh-zeh lî bĕḵōrāh — "Behold, I am about to die! What good is the birthright to me?" Esau's response elevates hunger to mortal threat — hôlēḵ lāmût ("going to die") is hyperbole, as the narrator will clarify in v34 by noting that Esau ate, drank, rose, and went his way. The rhetorical question wĕlāmmāh-zeh lî bĕḵōrāh expresses the logic of immediate gratification over long-term covenant inheritance. Esau is reasoning correctly within his own value framework: if you die, birthright has no value. The flaw is the premise — he is not dying.
33: hiššāḇĕʿāh lî kāyôm wayyiššāḇaʿ lô wayyimkōr ʾet-bĕḵōrātô lĕyaʿăqōḇ — "Swear to me today. And he swore to him, and sold his birthright to Jacob." The verb šāḇaʿ (H7650, to swear, to take an oath) derives from the root šeḇa (seven) — to swear was literally "to seven oneself" or to repeat a solemn formula seven times. An oath (šĕḇûʿāh) made the transaction legally binding and irrevocable under covenant norms — this is why Esau's subsequent attempts to recover the birthright are futile (27:36). Wayyimkōr (and he sold) — the completed sale; the birthright has legally transferred. The oath transforms what might have been a casual exchange into a permanent legal act.
34: wayyiḇez ʿēśāw ʾet-habbĕḵōrāh — "And Esau despised the birthright." The verb bāzāh (H959, to despise, disesteem, treat as worthless) — the narrator's verdict on Esau's action. The word is used for showing contempt for the covenant (Num 15:31, "despised the word of YHWH"), for despising a parent (Prov 15:20), and for despising YHWH himself (1 Sam 2:30; 2 Sam 12:10). The narrator does not say Jacob sinned by purchasing; the ethical verdict is entirely on Esau for his contempt of the covenantal birthright. This is the evaluation the NT picks up in Heb 12:16: Esau is called bébēlos (profane, common, secular) — one who treats the sacred as ordinary.
